author: @darrellpidgeon6440
text: Wow, another great use for conductive ink!  I like your original design, with the two solid triangles.  I wonder what would happen if you added a second one above the first, ninety degrees apart.  After making the proper connections, would that perhaps make it less directional?  I haven't turned on my tv in a very long time, but it would be nice to experiment, just for giggles.  lol.  Thanks!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
graham kaveman
it worked better than the bought unit - it had a wider operating range, could recieve a good signal lower down and had more channels on the scan - I actually had trouble with the shop unit when it came to tuning but the painted unit tuned in easily. if you just put homemade diy tv antenna in google loads will come up

author: @algirdaslazauskas2733
text:
The actual functional designs come from Mother Nature. On the year 2012 our analog TV have changed to DVB-T. I am an old school, so instead of buying one I have tested many aluminum pie forms. The one that worked (and still works amazing, better than commercial ones) me was the aluminum star pie form:
http://science.e-business.lt/uploads/10371711_10204197084151812_4030015594123346415_n.jpg
If you look closer, it assembles fractal geometry. All our mobile phones possess the antenna which has the appropriate fractal geometry to receive radio calls.    
Once you use the appropriate fractal geometry you can downsize (using carbon-based conductive materials) your TV antenna to 2x2 cm2 (so far I have managed) =)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i like that - it's just a matter of experimenting with various designs to optimise it

author: @bridgendesar
text:
Just looked on google for Bi-conical antenna, they don't have the two halves connected as you have with boarder of ink (the butterfly wings are always separate on the ones on the net), have you stumbled onto something?
Well, I have a balun on order and will be trying out your design using tin foil.
If you take back that £30 antenna, you could get a dvbt-t signal meter for about £11 on ebay (analogue meter looks nice), you won't need a tv licence to test in the lab either

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: oh ok - that's good news - i am forever stumbling about lol - i think i will pull the antenna apart and have a look at the guts now i have it - let me know what happens with the replication

replies.author: @PhattyMo
replies.text: TV license? That's still a thing over there? I thought they did away with that years (decades) ago. Seems ridiculous to me,but maybe that's how they pay all those BBC presenters. I wonder how much RF transmit power one of these conductive ink antennas could take,it might be useful for HAM radio operators. Paint a _insert_your_favorite_antenna_type_ on a wall,and start making contacts. You could fine-tune it by scraping off/adding a bit more paint to get it resonant.
